figures.caption: Figure 5. Additional results for IRS refinement dynamics: Visualization of pose hypotheses distribution over refinement rounds R ∈ {1, 2, 3, 4}, demonstrating convergence towards the ground truth and how hypothesis consensus reflects confidence.

Figure 4. Visualization of an initial, scattered distribution of N = 10 hypotheses is iteratively guided by our learned field over R = 4 rounds in the IRS algorithm. The population progressively converges into a tight cluster centered on the Ground Truth.

Table 1. KITTI test results comparison. We report localization error (Mean/Median, m), lateral/longitudinal recall (R@1m/R@5m, %), and inference speed (FPS) for both Same-Area and Cross-Area splits. Best results are in bold.

Figure 3. Qualitative examples on the VIGOR dataset. The trajectories demonstrate our model’s regression field, showing how five randomly sampled initial hypotheses are guided towards a coherent solution near the ground truth (red X) using our IRS algorithm.

Figure 2. The top panel illustrates the architecture of our proposed GeoFlow. Ground (Fg) and satellite (Fs) features are extracted by ψg and ψs, then flattened, positionally encoded, and fused by a Cross-Attention block to produce a single visual representation, fvis. Separately, an initial location point q0 = (x0, y0) is passed through a fully connected layer (FC). This point embedding is concatenated with fvis to form the joint representation s. Finally, two regression heads (vϕ) predict the parameters for the probabilistic displacement: distance (µr, σr) and direction (µθ, κ). The bottom panel illustrates our Iterative Refinement Sampling (IRS) algorithm. (1) The process starts with N randomly sampled hypotheses (red dots), the green dot represents the ground truth location. (2-4) In each round, our model predicts the displacement (blue arrows) for all hypotheses, which are then updated and iteratively converge toward the target. (5) After R rounds, the final robust location (yellow dot) is computed as the mean of the entire converged population.

Figure 8. Failure Cases: Localization difficulties in densely vegetated forest environments. The top row shows the ground images, and the bottom row visualizes the refinement trajectories and final converged hypotheses on the corresponding satellite images.
Table 2. Comparison on VIGOR dataset. Localization error (m) and inference speed (FPS) are reported. Best results are in bold.

Figure 7. Additional Qualitative localization examples by GeoFlow on the Cross-Area VIGOR dataset. The figure visualizes the trajectories generated by the learned regression field by GeoFlow: trajectories show how five randomly sampled initial pose hypotheses (seeds) are transformed to their respective predicted locations (colored circular markers), relative to the ground truth (red X).

Table 7. 3-DoF Pose Estimation Comparison on KITTI. GeoFlow demonstrates competitive accuracy against state-of-the-art methods while maintaining a superior computational profile. Best results are in bold

Figure 6. Additional Qualitative localization examples by GeoFlow on the Same-Area VIGOR dataset. The figure visualizes the trajectories generated by the learned regression field by GeoFlow: trajectories show how five randomly sampled initial pose hypotheses (seeds) are transformed to their respective predicted locations (colored circular markers), relative to the ground truth (red X).
